title: "ATAP Workshop: Folder Organisation"


output:

    toc: true

    code_folding: show
    highlight: pygments
---





# Introduction

Good folder organisation is one of the simplest but most important aspects of reproducible research. Structuring projects clearly makes it much easier to:

- navigate analyses
- locate outputs
- avoid overwriting files
- collaborate with others
- reproduce workflows later

Throughout this workshop we assume that analyses are being run inside an R Project. Start by creating a folder that can house all of this work along the lines of ATAP workshop. Open R and save a new project in this folder. 

Shooting from the hip in the beginning will come back to bite you as projects grow in complexity! 

::: {.columns}

::: {.column width="50%"}

### Ouch

![](plots/messy_folder.png)

:::

::: {.column width="50%"}

### Much better!

![](plots/clean_folder.png)

:::

:::

## Check your R version

Before starting, it is useful to check the version of R that you are using.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
R.Version()
```
:::




# Installing and Loading Packages

We will use the `pacman` package as a convenient way to install and load packages simultaneously.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
if (!requireNamespace("pacman", quietly = TRUE)) {
  install.packages("pacman")
}
```
:::




Load the required package:




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
pacman::p_load(purrr)
```
:::




# Working Directories

A working directory is the folder where R will look for files and save outputs by default.

Check your current working directory:




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
getwd()
```
:::




Because we are working inside an R Project, the working directory is automatically set to the project root when the project is opened. So you can simple navigate all your folders once created with "data/detections.csv" for example

# Creating Project Folders

A clean project structure helps separate:
- raw data
- scripts
- plots
- tables
- manuscript files

We first create a path object pointing to the project location.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
folder_path <- "."
```
:::




Because we are working inside an R Project, `"."` refers to the project root directory. Instead of hardcoding

## Define Folder Names

Choose the folders you want to create. Common examples include:
- `data`
- `scripts`
- `plots`
- `tables`
- `paper`

Remember:
- folder names are character strings and must be enclosed in quotation marks
- avoid accidental spaces in folder names




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
folders <- c(
  "data",
  "scripts",
  "plots",
  "tables",
  "paper"
)
```
:::




# Create the Folders

We can use `purrr::walk()` to iteratively create all folders in one call.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
purrr::walk(
  file.path(folder_path, folders),
  dir.create,
  recursive = TRUE,
  showWarnings = FALSE
)
```
:::




# Check the Folder Structure

Finally, confirm that the folders were created successfully.




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
list.dirs(folder_path, recursive = FALSE)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "./.quarto"     "./.Rproj.user" "./data"        "./docs"       
[5] "./paper"       "./plots"       "./scripts"     "./tables"     
```


:::
:::




Easy and now we can get stuck into it with peace of mind that things are easy to find.
